svg.text: ENGLISH + WELSH — RUNNING TEXT · PASSAGES
ALT DRAFT · SERIES 48D · REMEMBER FORWARD
— ENGLISH — PASSAGE 1 · SHAKESPEARE —
"What a piece of work is a man! How noble in reason, how infinite in faculty!"
— Hamlet, Act 2 Scene 2. William Shakespeare, ~1600.
— ENGLISH — PASSAGE 2 · PROVERB —
"A stitch in time saves nine." — Early intervention prevents greater effort later.
Earliest recorded ~1732. Reflects practical, future-oriented thinking in folk wisdom.
— ENGLISH — BRIDGE PHRASE · REMEMBER FORWARD —
This was made for you, freely, by people who remembered forward.
"Forward" in English: from Old English "foreweard" — toward what is in front.
"Future": from Latin futurus — "about to be." English borrowed it wholesale from Latin.
English uniquely has both Germanic (forthcoming) and Latin (future) words for what lies ahead.
WELSH (CYMRAEG) · RUNNING TEXT · PASSAGES
— WELSH — PASSAGE 1 · Y GODODDIN (~600 CE) —
"Ef aeth Catraeth, oedd ffraeth ei lu."
"He went to Catraeth, spirited was his host."
— Y Gododdin, Aneirin, ~600 CE. One of the oldest Welsh poems. Records a battle against Anglo-Saxons.
This text predates Beowulf and most Old English literature. Welsh literacy is ancient.
— WELSH — PASSAGE 2 · PROVERB —
"Heb iaith, heb galon."
Without language, without heart.
"Without language, without a heart." — Welsh proverb on the identity carried by a language.
— WELSH — BRIDGE PHRASE · REMEMBER FORWARD —
Cafodd hwn ei wneud i chi, yn rhad ac am ddim, gan bobl a gofiodd ymlaen.
Was-made this its-making to you, for free, by people who remembered forward.
"This was made for you, freely, by people who remembered forward."
— LANGUAGE NOTE — "FORWARD" IN WELSH —
ymlaen: "forward / onward" — from ym (intensive) + blaen (front/tip/point). Lit. "toward the point."
blaen: "front, tip, blade" — the forward-most part. The same root gives "bland" in some branches.
dyfodol: "future" — from dyfod (to come) + suffix -ol. Lit. "the coming thing." Future comes to you.
gofio: "to remember" — from co (memory) + intensifier. Cò is the Celtic root for memory across all branches.
— DECODING NOTE —
English: ~1.5B speakers. Lingua franca of science, aviation, diplomacy. SVO, Latin alphabet.
Welsh: ~750K speakers in Wales + diaspora. VSO, Celtic mutations, oldest literary tradition in Britain.
Welsh survived Roman occupation, Anglo-Saxon migration, Norman conquest, and English suppression.
The Welsh Language Act (1993) and the Welsh Language Measure (2011) gave Welsh co-official status in Wales.
PLATE 48D ALT · ENGLISH/WELSH · TEXT · CC BY-SA 4.0 · REMEMBERFORWARD.ORG
THIS IS A PATIENT MESSAGE. IT WAS MADE FOR YOU, FREELY, BY PEOPLE WHO REMEMBERED FORWARD.
BUY IT · BUILD IT · BURY IT · FOR SOMEONE YOU WILL NEVER MEET
